   9.  Each motorcycle card component should have an element with a class of `motorcycle-card-price` that lists the price of the motorcycle in plain text. 
   10. Each motorcycle card component should have an element with a class of `motorcycle-card-engine` that lists the horsepower of each engine.
6. You  should have a class called `MotorcycleGalleryApp`. 
   1. The class should have a `private` array called `allMotorcycles` that is statically typed to a type of an array of `Motorcycle` objects. 
   2. That class should have a function called `renderMotorcycles` that renders the equivalent motorcycle card components of the `allMotorcycles` variable inside the element with id of `motorcycle-grid`. 
7. The number of `Motorcycle` elements that are displayed should be listed inside of an element with an id of `results-number`.
8.  The filter should be updated on an `input` event.

challenges.title: What Are Generics, and How Do They Work?
What Is Type Narrowing, and How Does It Work?
Step 1
challenges.description:
In this workshop, you'll learn about TypeScript abstract classes and generics by building a bug species selector that displays different bug emojis.

The HTML and CSS for the project have been provided for you so you can focus on the TypeScript. You can check the files to learn about the elements you will select later.

To start, you'll need an abstract base class for all bugs. Abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly and are meant to be extended by other classes.

Create an abstract class named `Bug` with a generic type parameter `T`.

challenges.title: Step 2
challenges.description:
Now you need to define the properties that all bugs will have.

Inside the `Bug` class, declare a property named `emoji` with the type `T` using the definite assignment assertion operator (`!`). This tells TypeScript that the property will be assigned later.

challenges.title: Step 3
challenges.description: Still inside the `Bug` class, declare a property named `emojiElement` with the type `HTMLParagraphElement`. This property will hold a reference to the HTML paragraph element where the bug emoji will be displayed.

challenges.title: Step 4
challenges.description: Next, create a constructor for the `Bug` class that takes a single parameter named `emojiElement` of type `HTMLParagraphElement`. Inside the constructor, assign the `emojiElement` parameter to the `emojiElement` property of the class.

challenges.title: Step 5
challenges.description: After the constructor, create an abstract `render` method that has a return type of `void`. You'll use this in subclasses to update the displayed emoji.

challenges.title: Step 6
challenges.description: Next, create a subclass named `Bee` that extends the `Bug` class with the generic type parameter set to `string`.

challenges.title: Step 7
challenges.description:
Inside the `Bee` class, create a constructor that takes a single parameter `emojiElement` of type `HTMLParagraphElement`. 

Inside the constructor, call the `super` function with the `emojiElement` parameter to invoke the constructor of the parent `Bug` class. Then, set the `emoji` property to the string value `"🐝"`.

challenges.title: Step 8
challenges.description: Inside the `Bee` class, implement the `render` method, prefixed by the `override` keyword, to update the `innerText` property of the `emojiElement` with the value of the `emoji` property.
tests:
const explorer = await __helpers.Explorer(code);
assert.isTrue(explorer.classes.Bee.methods.render.matches("override render() { this.emojiElement.innerText = this.emoji; }"));

challenges.title: Step 9
challenges.description: Create a subclass `Spider` that extends the `Bug` class with the generic type parameter set to `string`.

challenges.title: Step 10
challenges.description:
Inside the `Spider` class, create a constructor that takes a single parameter `emojiElement` of type `HTMLParagraphElement`. 

Inside the constructor, call the `super` function with the `emojiElement` parameter to invoke the constructor of the parent `Bug` class. Then, set the `emoji` property to the string value `"🕷️"`.

challenges.title: Step 11
challenges.description: Inside the `Spider` class, implement the `render` method, prefixed by the `override` keyword, to update the `innerText` property of the `emojiElement` with the value of the `emoji` property.
tests:
const explorer = await __helpers.Explorer(code);
assert.isTrue(explorer.classes.Spider.methods.render.matches("override render() { this.emojiElement.innerText = this.emoji; }"));

challenges.title: Step 12
challenges.description: Create a function named `isSelect` that takes a single parameter `element` of type `EventTarget | null`. This function will check if the selected element is a `<select>` HTML element.

challenges.title: Step 13
challenges.description: Now inside the `isSelect` function, return a boolean indicating whether the `element` is an instance of `HTMLSelectElement`. Use the `instanceof` operator to perform this check.

challenges.title: Step 14
challenges.description:
You will now start referencing the needed DOM elements.

Get the paragraph element with the ID of `bug-emoji` and store it in a constant named `bugEmojiElement`. Use the `!` non-null assertion operator to assert that the element is not `null`.

challenges.title: Step 15
challenges.description: Next, define an object named `bugMap` with the type `Record<string, Bug<string>>` and assign it an empty object for now.

challenges.title: Step 16
challenges.description: Now populate `bugMap` with two entries. Add `"bee"` as a key mapped to a new `Bee` instance, and `"spider"` as a key mapped to a new `Spider` instance. Pass in `bugEmojiElement` as the argument of both.

challenges.title: Step 17
challenges.description: Get the `select` element with ID `species` from the DOM and store it in a constant named `selectElement`. Use the `!` non-null assertion operator to assert that the element is not `null`

challenges.title: Step 18
challenges.description:
Up until now, nothing changes when you select a different bug.

Add a `change` event listener to `selectElement`. In the callback, use `isSelect` to check if `e.target` is an `HTMLSelectElement`. You don't need to do anything inside the `if` block yet.

challenges.title: Step 19
challenges.description:
Now that the `if` check is in place, use `e.target.value` to look up the corresponding bug in `bugMap` and call its `render` method. After that, you should be able to select the bugs and see the result right away.

With that, your Bug Emoji Picker workshop is complete!

challenges.title: Build a Product Showcase
challenges.description:
For this lab, you have been provided with all the HTML and CSS. You will complete a product showcase using TypeScript.

**Objective:** Fulfill the user stories below and get all the tests to pass to complete the lab.

**User Stories:**

1. You should create an interface named `Item` with:
   - A `type` property that can only be `"book"`, `"electronics"`, or `"clothing"`.
   - An `id` property of type `string`.
   - A `price` property of type `number`.

1. You should create an interface named `Book` that extends `Item`. Its `type` property should be set to the literal `"book"`. It should also have `title` and `author` properties of type `string`.

1. You should create an interface named `Electronics` that extends `Item`. Its `type` property should be set to the literal `"electronics"`. It should also have `item` and `model` properties of type `string`, and an optional `warranty` property of type `number`.

1. You should create an interface named `Clothing` that extends `Item`. Its `type` property should be set to the literal `"clothing"`. It should also have `item` and `brand` properties of type `string`, and an optional `size` property that can only be `"S"`, `"M"`, or `"L"`.

1. You should create a union type named `Product` that combines the `Book`, `Electronics`, and `Clothing` interfaces.

1. You should create a generic class named `Collection` with a type parameter `T`. The constructor should accept an array of items of type `T` and store them in a property named `items`.

1. The `Collection` class should have a `getAll()` method that returns all items in the collection.

1. The `Collection` class should have a `filter()` method that accepts a callback and returns only the elements from the `items` array that satisfy the given condition. The callback should receive an element of type `T` and return a boolean.

1. You should create a function named `renderProduct` with a parameter of type `Product`. It should return an HTML string representing a product with:
   - An element with the class `item` and an ID with the value of the product `id`.
   - An element with the class `price` and the text of the product `price`.
   - An element containing additional product information.

1. The `renderProduct` function should use type narrowing to render different HTML depending on the product type:

   - For books, the rendered HTML should have the format `Book: [title] by [author]`, where `[title]` and `[author]` should be replaced by the title and the author, respectively.
   - For electronics, the rendered HTML should have the format `Electronics: [item] - [model]`. If the product has a warranty, append ` - Warranty: [warranty] year(s)` to the format. Replace `[item]`, `[model]`, and `[warranty]` with the item, the model, and the warranty, respectively.
   - For clothing, the rendered HTML should have the format `Clothing: [item] by [brand]`. If the product has a size, append ` - Size [size]` to the format. Replace `[item]`, `[brand]`, and `[size]` with the item, the brand, and the size, respectively.
   - If an invalid product type is encountered, throw an error with the format: `Unknown product type: [product]`, where `[product]` should be replaced by the invalid product object. Use `JSON.stringify` to convert the invalid product into a string.

1. You should create a `Collection<Product>` instance that includes at least one item for each product type and assign the instance to a variable named `products`.

1. You should create a `showProducts` function that displays products in the `#output` element. It should accept an optional filter representing a product type; when omitted, show all products; when provided, show only products of that type.

1. You should render products by converting each product to an HTML string using the `renderProduct` function and injecting the combined result into the `#output` element.

1. Clicking the `#all`, `#books`, `#electronics`, and `#clothing` buttons should display the corresponding set of products in the `#output` element.

1. On page load, all products should be shown by default.
tests:
const explorer = await __helpers.Explorer(code);
assert.isTrue(explorer.interfaces.Electronics.hasTypeProps({name: "warranty", type: "number", isOptional: true}));

challenges.title: What Is a tsconfig File, and Why Is It Important to Include in Your TypeScript Projects?
challenges.description:
TypeScript's compiler settings can be configured to meet your project's needs. That configuration lives in a `tsconfig.json` file in the root directory of your project. In fact, without it, the compiler will not run unless you pass it command flags directly. But what exactly does this file do? Well, let's take a look at an example file:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "rootDir": "./src",
    "outDir": "./prod",
    "lib": ["ES2023"],
    "target": "ES2023",
    "module": "ES2022",
    "moduleResolution": "Node",
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "strict": true
  },
  "exclude": ["test/"]
}
```

This seems like a lot! So let's break it down. The `compilerOptions` property is going to contain the "meat" of your configuration - this is where you control how the TypeScript compiler behaves. Looking at that nested object…

The `rootDir` and `outDir` tell TypeScript which directory holds your source files, and which directory should contain the transpiled JavaScript code.

The `lib` property determines which type definitions the compiler uses, and allows you to include support for specific ES releases, the DOM, and more.

`module` and `moduleResolution` effectively work in tandem to manage how your package uses modules - either CommonJS or ECMAScript.

`esModuleInterop` allows for smoother interoperability between CommonJS and ES modules by automatically creating namespace objects for imports, making it easier to use modules from different systems together in your TypeScript projects, and the `skipLibCheck` option skips validating `.d.ts` files that aren't referenced by imports in your code.

And finally we reach the `strict` mode. One might argue that TypeScript isn't truly helpful without this flag enabled, as it toggles quite a few other checks, such as requiring you to properly handle nullable types, or warn when TypeScript can't infer a type and falls back to any.

Before we finish, a quick note about the top-level `exclude` property - when you've defined a source directory, you may have TypeScript code outside of that directory which you don't want compiled as part of your production code. For example, your test code. The `exclude` array tells the compiler to ignore these TypeScript files during compilation, but still allows tooling like Intellisense to expose potential issues.

There are a ton of other compiler options you can explore - over 50! I encourage you to explore the documentation and experiment to find the configuration that works for your project's needs.

challenges.title: Step 1
challenges.description:
In this workshop, you will continue to practice working with TypeScript by building a fortune telling app.

Begin by declaring an interface named `Card`. Inside the `Card` interface, add the following properties: `name`, `name_short` and `value`. The `name`, and `name_short` properties should have the types set to `string` and the `value` should have a union type set to `string | number`.
tests:
const explorer = await __helpers.Explorer(code);
assert.isTrue(explorer.interfaces.Card.hasTypeProps([{ name: "value", type: "string | number" }]));

challenges.title: Step 2
challenges.description: There are more properties to add to the `Card` interface. Start by setting `value_int` to type `number`. All other properties should be of type `string`: `suit`, `type`, `img`, `meaning_up`, `meaning_rev`, and `desc`.

challenges.title: Step 3
challenges.description: You need another interface called `Deck`. This interface should have a `cards` property, which is an array of the previous interface.

challenges.title: Step 4
challenges.description:
In a previous lecture, you learned that type inference is TypeScript's ability to automatically determine and assign types to variables, function returns, and expressions based on their usage and context.

In TypeScript, generics make functions, classes, and types more flexible. Generics allow the creation of type variables, eliminating the need to explicitly define types each time. This enables the same function to work safely with different types.

Here is a simple example of generics:

 ```ts
 function Items<T>(list:T[]): T[] {
    return list
 }

Items<string | number>(["apple", 12,"orange"]) // here the argument is a union type
```

Functions can use two or more generics. `T` is used by convention, and additional type variables like `S` can be added as needed.

```ts
const SayHello = <S, T>(v1: S, v2: T): [S, T] => {
  return [v1, v2]
}

SayHello<string, string>("I'm", "alive") // ["I'm", "alive"]
SayHello<string, number>("I'm", 20)     // ["I'm", 20]
```

Create a function `getElement` that takes `selector` of type `string` as a parameter. It should also take a generic type `T` that extends `HTMLElement`, and have a return type `T`.

challenges.title: Step 5
challenges.description: Inside the `getElement` function, use `querySelector` with the generics and store the result in a variable. Add an `if` statement to check if the element does not exist, and if so, throw an error with the message `Element not found: ${error message here}`. If the element exists, return it.

challenges.title: Step 6
challenges.description:
Next, you need to create a function to hide elements from the DOM. Declare a `hideElements` function that takes `...elements` as a parameter with the type `HTMLElement[]`.

This function, with a CSS `hidden` class, will be used throughout the app to toggle the visibility of UI elements.

challenges.title: Step 7
challenges.description: Wrap up the function by using the `forEach` array method to iterate over the elements argument. Inside the callback function, add the hidden class to each element by using the `.add()` method of `classList`.

challenges.title: Step 8
challenges.description: You need another function that shows elements in the DOM. Similar to the last function, create a function named `showElements`. This time use `classList.remove("hidden")`.

challenges.title: Step 9
challenges.description:
So far, you have three functions: `getElement`, `hideElements`, and `showElements`.
Now you need a function that helps you get a random fortune.

Declare a function named `getRandomItem` that takes an array of `items` as an argument. Use generics for this function.

Inside the function, use `Math.random()` and `Math.floor()` to select a random fortune from the `items` array.
Don’t forget to return the selected fortune.

challenges.title: Step 10
challenges.description:
Since the project now works with HTML DOM elements, you need a way to render the fortune to the DOM.

Declare a function named `renderCard` that takes four arguments: `drawingType`, `isReversed`, `shortName`, and `img`.

The parameters `drawingType`, `shortName`, and `img` must be of type `string`, while `isReversed` must be of type `boolean`. The function should have a return type of `string`.

challenges.title: Step 11
challenges.description: Now, inside the template literal of the `renderCard` function, create a `div` element. Inside the `div`, add an `h2` element that outputs `drawingType`, and a `figure` element with the class `card_container ${isReversed ? "reversed-card" : ""}` and the attribute `data-id="${shortName}"`.

challenges.title: Step 12
challenges.description:
A default image for the card and the URL to make an API request to have been provided, you only need some variables to save them in.

Create a `CDN_URL` variable and set it to `https://cdn.freecodecamp.org/curriculum/typescript/tarot-app`. Also create a `defaultImg` variable with the value `new URL("default.svg", CDN_URL + "/")`, and a `LOCAL_DEFAULT_IMG` variable with the value `defaultImg`.
tests:
const explorer = await __helpers.Explorer(code);
assert.isTrue(explorer.variables.defaultImg.matches('const defaultImg = new URL("default.svg", CDN_URL + "/")'));

challenges.title: Step 13
challenges.description: Now, add a `div` with the class `img-loader`. After that, create an `img` element and give it a conditional URL for the image using ``${img ? `${CDN_URL}/${img}` : LOCAL_DEFAULT_IMG}``.

challenges.title: Step 14
challenges.description: Now, use the `onload` event to remove the `hidden` class when the image loads and hide the loading placeholder using its `previousElementSibling`.

challenges.title: Step 15
challenges.description: Add an `onerror` handler to the image element to handle loading failures. On the first failure, mark it using `dataset.failed` and replace the source with the local default image. If the image fails again, remove the hidden class and hide the loading placeholder using `previousElementSibling`.

challenges.title: Step 16
challenges.description: Since the fortune app only has three moments Past, Present, and Future, declare an enum named `DrawingType` with three properties: `Past`, `Present`, and `Future`. Each property should have a string value corresponding to its name.

challenges.title: Step 17
challenges.description:
Now you need a main class that will help render the UI dynamically in the DOM.

Create a class named `Game`. Inside it, add a property called `cards` of type `Card[]`, and for now, initialize it with an empty array.

challenges.title: Step 18
challenges.description: Inside the class, add a private `elements` property as an object. Inside it, add the properties `singleCardBtn`, `singleCard`, and `multipleCardsBtn`, each typed as `HTMLElement`.

challenges.title: Step 19
challenges.description: Now add the remaining properties to the private `elements` object. They are: `multipleCard`, `title`, `newReadingBtn`, `fortuneContainer`, `fortuneDescription`, `headerTitle`, `subTitle`, `cardTitle`, `description`, and `text`. Make sure each property uses the `HTMLElement` type.

challenges.title: Step 20
challenges.description:
Add a `constructor` inside the class. In the constructor, you need to use the elements you created earlier to select the corresponding DOM elements using the `getElement` function.

For now, handle `singleCardBtn` and `singleCard`. For `singleCardBtn`, pass in `#btn-single-card`, and for `singleCard`, pass in the `.single_card`.

challenges.title: Step 21
challenges.description:
Now, do the same for the rest of the properties. Use the `getElement` function to select each one:

| Property | Selector | 
| ---------- | ---------- |
| `multipleCardsBtn` | `#btn-multiple-cards` | 
| `multipleCard` | `.multiple_card` | 
| `title` | `.title` | 
| `newReadingBtn` | `.btn_reveal` | 
| `fortuneContainer` | `.fortune_container` | 
| `fortuneDescription` | `.fortune_description` | 
| `headerTitle` | `.header_title` | 
| `subTitle` | `.sub_title` | 
| `cardTitle` | `.desc_title` | 
| `description` | `.description` | 
| `text` | `.text` |

challenges.title: Step 22
challenges.description:
Inside the constructor, add calls to `fetchCardsData()` and `initializeEventListeners()` using the `this` keyword, so they run when the class is initialized.

The `fetchCardsData` method will handle loading the tarot card data from an API, and `initializeEventListeners` will set up the button click handlers. You'll create both methods in the coming steps.

challenges.title: Step 23
challenges.description: Since you're calling `fetchCardsData` and `initializeEventListeners` inside the constructor, you now need to define them inside the class. Create both as `private` methods. Make sure `fetchCardsData` is asynchronous since it will handle API calls.

challenges.title: Step 24
challenges.description:
Inside the `fetchCardsData` method, declare a variable named `response` and assign it by calling the `fetch` function with the following URL: `` `${CDN_URL}/card_data.json` ``.

Add the `await` keyword before the `fetch` call, and wrap the entire logic inside a `try...catch` block.

challenges.title: Step 25
challenges.description: After fetching the data, store the response in a variable and parse it to JSON. Give the parsed data the type `Deck`, and then assign it to the `cards` property. Inside the `catch` block, make sure to log the error with this message: `"Error fetching cards:", error`.

challenges.title: Step 26
challenges.description: Now you will start working on the `initializeEventListeners()` method. First, give it the return type `void`.

challenges.title: Step 27
challenges.description:
Inside the `initializeEventListeners` method, attach a `click` event listener to `singleCardBtn`. Within the event handler, call the `singleCardSelected` method. remember to get the `singleCardBtn` from `elements`.

You will create this method in the coming steps.

challenges.title: Step 28
challenges.description:
Now do the same for `multipleCardsBtn` and `newReadingBtn` by attaching a click event listener to `multipleCardsBtn` that calls `multipleCardSelected`, and another to `newReadingBtn` that calls `newReading`.

You will create the two methods in the coming steps.

challenges.title: Step 29
challenges.description:
Add another event listener to the global `document` object that calls the `showFortune` function. Inside the callback, include a parameter `e` of type `Event`, and pass `e` as an argument to the `showFortune` method.

`showFortune` will handle card click events, like reading the clicked card ID from the DOM and displaying the matching card fortune details. You will create it in the coming steps.

challenges.title: Step 30
challenges.description:
Now you will start creating the methods. 

Create a method called `singleCardSelected`. Inside it, call the `hideElements` function and pass the following properties: `singleCardBtn`, `multipleCardsBtn`, `multipleCard`, `text`, and `headerTitle`.

Remember you must access all of them using `this.elements`.

challenges.title: Step 31
challenges.description:
In some cases, the cards need to be reversed, since they are not always in the correct position.

Declare a variable called `isReversed` inside the `singleCardSelected` method. Use the `Math.random()` method to generate a random boolean value. To ensure the value stays within bounds, compare it with `0.5`.

challenges.title: Step 32
challenges.description: The fortune must be random, so a helper function is required to select a random fortune. Declare a variable named `chosenCard` inside the `singleCardSelected` method. Use the `getRandomItem` method to obtain a random card and pass `cards` as an argument.

challenges.title: Step 33
challenges.description: Within the `singleCardSelected` method, retrieve the `singleCard` element and update its `innerHTML` by calling the `renderCard` function. Pass `"Click the card and reveal the fortune"` as the first argument, and `isReversed` as the second argument.

challenges.title: Step 34
challenges.description: Include the remaining arguments `chosenCard.name_short` and `chosenCard.img`. Then outside the `renderCard` function, reset the `multipleCard` element's `innerHTML` to an empty string.

challenges.title: Step 35
challenges.description: Right after you clear `multipleCard`, inside the `singleCardSelected` method, call the `showElements` function and pass `singleCard` as the first argument and `fortuneContainer` as the second argument.

challenges.title: Step 36
challenges.description:
The fortune-teller app supports rendering multiple cards, so you need to have a method that can help render multiple cards. Declare a method called `multipleCardSelected`.

Inside the method, call the `hideElements` function and pass in the properties `singleCard`, `singleCardBtn`, `multipleCardsBtn`, and `headerTitle`.vDon't forget you have to access them from `this.elements`.

challenges.title: Step 37
challenges.description: Now call the `showElements` function and pass in `multipleCard`, `fortuneContainer`, and `text` as properties.

challenges.title: Step 38
challenges.description: As done for the previous `multipleCard`, get the `multipleCard` element again and set its `innerHTML` by iterating through `DrawingType`. Use `Object.values()` and call the `map` method on it, passing `type` as an argument.

challenges.title: Step 39
challenges.description: Again, you need to make the card random and reversed. Declare a variable named `isReversed` and `card`. For the first one, call the `Math.random` method and compare it with `< 0.5`. For the second one, call `getRandomItem` and pass `this.cards`.

challenges.title: Step 40
challenges.description: Inside the `map`, return the `renderCard` function by passing `type`, `isReversed`, `name_short`, and `img` as arguments, and add the `join` method on the `map` with an empty string.

challenges.title: Step 41
challenges.description: Now add another method called `showFortune`. As a parameter, pass `e` and give it the type `Event`. Also create a `newReading` method and leave it empty for now.

challenges.title: Step 42
challenges.description:
You should test your progress so far. Create an instance of the `Game` class and assign it to a `game` variable.

At this point, you'll see that you can see the cards when you click the Single Card Reading and
Three Card Spread buttons.

challenges.title: Step 43
challenges.description: Inside `showFortune` method declare a variable called `target` and assign it to `e.target` and create another empty if statement.

challenges.title: Step 44
challenges.description: For the condition of the `if` statement, check if the `target` is not an instance of `HTMLElement`. Then inside it, add a `return`.

challenges.title: Step 45
challenges.description: Declare a variable named `cardElement` and use the `card_container` class to get the element. Use the `closest` selector to get it from the `target`.

challenges.title: Step 46
challenges.description: Check if `cardElement` is not an instance of `HTMLElement`, and if so, add a `return`

challenges.title: Step 47
challenges.description: Check if `cardElement` is falsy, and if so, return without showing any message.

challenges.title: Step 48
challenges.description: Declare a variable named `cardId`, and assign it the value of the `data-id` attribute from `cardElement` using the `getAttribute()` method.

challenges.title: Step 49
challenges.description: Declare another variable named `foundCard`, and assign it to the result of calling the `find()` method on `cards`. Pass in `card` as the callback parameter, and compare `card.name_short` with `cardId` to find the matching card using strict equality.

challenges.title: Step 50
challenges.description: Add an `if` statement to check whether `foundCard` exists before updating the elements with its data or content block.

challenges.title: Step 51
challenges.description: Inside the `if` statement, get the `cardTitle` element and set its `textContent` to `foundCard.name`. Do the same for the `description` element by setting its `textContent` to `foundCard.desc`.

challenges.title: Step 52
challenges.description: Now do the same for `subTitle` by assigning it to `meaning_up`, and for `title` assign it to `foundCard.name`. After that, call the `showElements` method and pass `fortuneDescription` from the elements.

challenges.title: Step 53
challenges.description:
At this point, you should be able to see the fortune each card shows once you click a card. The only thing left to do is to make the new reading button work.

Inside the `newReading` method, call `showElements()` and `hideElements()`.

challenges.title: Step 54
challenges.description: Now inside the `showElements`, pass these as arguments: `singleCardBtn`, `multipleCardsBtn`, and `headerTitle`. Remember you get them from the `elements`, and access them with the `this` keyword.

challenges.title: Step 55
challenges.description:
Finally, inside the `hideElements`, pass the arguments `singleCard`, `multipleCard`, `fortuneContainer`, and `fortuneDescription`. Remember to get them from the elements.

With that, your Fortune Telling app is complete!

challenges.title: Step 1
challenges.description:
In this workshop, you will build a FIFA-style football player card builder using React and TypeScript syntax extension (TSX), the TypeScript equivalent of JSX.

Remember from JSX that you write HTML-like elements directly inside JavaScript. One important difference from regular HTML is that you use `className` instead of `class` and `htmlFor` for CSS class and for attributes. This is because `class` and `for` are reserved keywords in JavaScript.

The CSS for the project has been provided for you so you can focus on the TypeScript part. You can take a look at it as you will be using classes from it.

Change the `h1` element to the text `Football Card Builder`.

challenges.title: Step 2
challenges.description: Create and export a function component called `PlayerCard` above the `FootballPlayerCard` component. Inside it, add a `return` statement. And in the `return` add a `div` with the className `card-wrapper` and `tier-gold`.

challenges.title: Step 3
challenges.description: Inside the `FootballPlayerCard` component, remove the `h1` and return the `PlayerCard` component.

challenges.title: Step 4
challenges.description:
The `div` element with class `card-wrapper` creates the outer border and clipped shape of the card. 

Now add the inner `div` directly inside `card-wrapper` with the `className` of `card`. This gives the card its dark background.

challenges.title: Step 5
challenges.description: Inside the `card` div, add a `div` with the `className` of `card-header`. This section will hold the player rating, position, tier badge, and club name.

challenges.title: Step 6
challenges.description: Add another `div` inside the `.card-header` element. This one won't have any `className`. Inside that `div`, add another `div` with the `className` of `card-rating` and the text `98`.

challenges.title: Step 7
challenges.description: Below the `card-rating` div, add a `div` with `className` of `card-position` and the text `ST`, standing for striker.

challenges.title: Step 8
challenges.description: After the `div` with no `className` of, add a `div` with `className` of `card-header-right`. Inside it, add a `div` with `className` of `card-tier-badge` and the text `GOLD`.

challenges.title: Step 9
challenges.description: Below the `div` element with the `className` of `card-tier-badge`, add a `div` with `className` of `card-club` and the text `Santos FC`.

challenges.title: Step 10
challenges.description:
The card header is complete. Now you need to add the player photo section.

After the closing `div` of `card-header` and still inside the `div` with `className` of `card`, add a `div` with the `className` of `card-image-wrap`. Leave it empty for now. You will add the image in the next step.

challenges.title: Step 11
challenges.description: Inside `card-image-wrap`, add an `img` element with an `src` of `https://cdn.freecodecamp.org/curriculum/typescript/tsx-workshop/pele.jpg`, an `alt` of `Edson Arantes do Nascimento (Pele) headshot`, and a `className` of `card-image`. Don't forget from JSX that elements without children must be self-closed with `/>`. So instead of ``, you should write ``. Same thing applies to TSX.

challenges.title: Step 12
challenges.description: After the `card-image-wrap` element, add a `div` with the `className` of `card-name-strip`. Inside it, add a `span` with the `className` of `card-name` and the text `PELE`.

challenges.title: Step 13
challenges.description: Now add a `div` with the `className` of `card-stats`. Inside it, add a `div` with the `className` of `stat-col`. Inside that, add three `div` elements with each with the `className` of `stat-row`.

challenges.title: Step 14
challenges.description: Add two `span` elements with the `className` of `stat-value` and `stat-label` respectively to each `div.stat-row`.

challenges.title: Step 15
challenges.description:
The `span` elements in the three rows should have these values:

- `97` and `PAC`
- `98` and `SHO`
- `83` and `PAS`

`PAC` stands for pace, `SHO` represents shot, and `PAS`represents passing.

The number goes in the `stat-value` spans and the attributes in the `stat-label` spans.

challenges.title: Step 16
challenges.description:
Inside `card-stats` div, after the first `stat-col`, you need to add two more children.

First, a self-closing `div` with the `className` of `stat-divider` to render a thin vertical line between the two stat columns, and a second `div` with the `className` of `stat-col` that contains three more `div` elements each with the `className` of `stat-row`.

challenges.title: Step 17
challenges.description: Add two `span` elements with the `className` of `stat-value` and `stat-label` respectively to each `stat-row`.

challenges.title: Step 18
challenges.description:
Each `stat-row` should contain two `span` elements with the `className` of `stat-value` and `stat-label` respectively.

In the `spans` elements inside the `div` elements, you should have the following:

- `99` and `DRI`
- `41` and `DEF`
- `75` and `PHY`

`DRI` stands for dribbling, `DEF` for defending, and `PHY` for physique.

challenges.title: Step 19
challenges.description:
The `PlayerCard` component works but uses hardcoded values. To make it flexible, you need to describe the shape of a player data using a TypeScript interface.

Before the `PlayerCard` function, create an interface called `PlayerData` with a `name` of type `string`, `overallRating` of type `number`, `position` of type `string`, and `club` of type `string`.

challenges.title: Step 20
challenges.description:
The `PlayerData` interface currently only describes basic player info. You need to extend it with the remaining properties your card needs.

Inside the interface, add `imageUrl` of type `string`, and six numeric stats: `pac`, `sho`, `pas`, `dri`, `def`, and `phy`, all of type `number`.

challenges.title: Step 21
challenges.description:
Before the `PlayerCard` function, define and export a function called `getPlayerTier` that takes a `rating` parameter of type `number` and returns a `string`.

The function should use `if` statements to return the appropriate tier label:

- return `elite` if `rating` is `92` or above
- return `gold` if `rating` is `85` or above
- return `silver` if `rating` is `75` or above
- return `bronze` otherwise

challenges.title: Step 22
challenges.description:
Now that the `PlayerData` interface is defined, update the `PlayerCard` component to accept a `player` prop typed as `PlayerData` to match the `PlayerData` interface.

Remember you can destructure props like this:

```js
({ user }: { user: UserData })
```
tests:
const explorer = await __helpers.Explorer(code);
assert.isTrue(explorer.allFunctions.PlayerCard.parameters[0].matches("{ player }: { player: PlayerData }"));

challenges.title: Step 23
challenges.description:
In the `PlayerCard` being referenced inside `FootballPlayerCard`, accept the `player` prop and pass in an object with the following properties for now:

| Key  | Value |
|---|---|
|  name  | PELE  |
|  overallRating  | 98  |
|  position |  ST |
|  club |  Santos FC |
|  imageUrl  |  https://cdn.freecodecamp.org/curriculum/typescript/tsx-workshop/pele.jpg  |
|  pac |  97  |
|  sho |  98  |
|  pas |  83  |
|  dri |  99  |
|  def |  41  |
|  phy |  75  |

challenges.title: Step 24
challenges.description:
In JSX and TSX, you pass JavaScript expressions as attribute values using curly braces. Template literals use backticks and `${}` for interpolation. For example:

```tsx
<div className={`prefix-${someVariable}`}>
```

The card wrapper `className` is currently hardcoded as `card-wrapper tier-gold`. Replace `gold` with a dynamic value by using a template literal that calls `getPlayerTier` with `player.overallRating`. This will reference the correct tier class from the CSS, based on the player's overall rating.

challenges.title: Step 25
challenges.description:
Remember from JSX that curly braces let you embed any JavaScript expression directly inside JSX. This is how you display dynamic data from props. You do the same in TSX.

The card header still shows hardcoded values. Replace the text `98` inside `card-rating` with `player.overallRating`, and replace `ST` inside `card-position` with `player.position`.

challenges.title: Step 26
challenges.description:
The tier badge still shows the hardcoded text `GOLD`. You need to replace it with a dynamic expression so the correct tier will show.

Call `getPlayerTier()` with `player.overallRating` to get the tier string, then call `.toUpperCase()` on the result to display it in uppercase. Don't forget to wrap the whole expression in curly braces.

challenges.title: Step 27
challenges.description: Replace the hardcoded club name of `Santos FC` in `card-club` with `player.club`.

challenges.title: Step 28
challenges.description:
The player photo is loaded from a hardcoded URL. Replace the `src` attribute value `https://cdn.freecodecamp.org/curriculum/typescript/tsx-workshop/pele.jpg` with `player.imageUrl`.

Also replace the hardcoded `alt` text with a dynamic value using a template literal with `player.name` and the text `headshot`.

Note that when you use a JavaScript expression as an attribute value in TSX, you use curly braces `{}` instead of quotes. For example `src={player.imageUrl}`, not `src="{player.imageUrl}"`.

challenges.title: Step 29
challenges.description: Replace the hardcoded player name `PELE` inside `card-name` with `player.name`.

challenges.title: Step 30
challenges.description:
The final hardcoded values are the six player stats. Replace the first three set of hardcoded numbers with the corresponding `player` property:

- `97` → `player.pac`
- `98` → `player.sho`
- `83` → `player.pas`

challenges.title: Step 31
challenges.description:
Now replace the remaining hardcoded numbers with the following:

- `99` → `player.dri`
- `41` → `player.def`
- `75` → `player.phy`

challenges.title: Step 32
challenges.description:
`PlayerCard` is now fully driven by props, but `FootballPlayerCard` passes the player data inline as a long object literal. Clean this up by defining a named constant.

Above `FootballPlayerCard`, create a constant named `defaultPlayer` with the type `PlayerData`. Move the keys and values from the inline `player` prop into this object.

challenges.title: Step 33
challenges.description: Now update `FootballPlayerCard` to pass `defaultPlayer` to `PlayerCard` instead of the inline object.

challenges.title: Step 34
challenges.description:
`FootballPlayerCard` currently renders the card with a fixed `defaultPlayer`. To make the app interactive, you need to manage the player data in React state so that updates can trigger re-renders.

Inside the `FootballPlayerCard` component, create a state variable called `player` with a setter `setPlayer` that will hold the current player data. Initialize this state with `defaultPlayer`, and specify that the state uses the `PlayerData` type as a generic so TypeScript knows what shape the player object should have.

After that, update the return to pass the `player` state variable as the prop to `PlayerCard`.

challenges.title: Step 35
challenges.description:
`FootballPlayerCard` currently renders only the bare card. To build a full page, add a `div` element with the `className` of `page`.

Inside that `div`, add a `header` element of `className` of `header`. Inside the `header`, add a `div` of `className` of `header-inner`. Inside the `header-inner` element, add two paragraphs of text `Football Card Builder` and `Customize your player card` respectively. The paragraphs should also have the `className` of `header-title` and `header-subtitle` each.

After the closing `header` element but still inside the `div` with `className` of `page`, move in the `PlayerCard` component with its prop as a temporary placeholder until the main layout is added.

challenges.title: Step 36
challenges.description:
Now wrap the `PlayerCard` component in a `main` element of `className` of `main`.

Inside the `main` element, add `div` of `className` of `layout`. Inside `layout`, add two separate `div` elements of `className` of `form-panel` and `preview-panel` respectively.

challenges.title: Step 37
challenges.description:
Inside the `preview-panel` element, add a `p` element of `className` of `preview-label` and text `Live Preview`, another `p` element of  `className` of `preview-hint` and the text `Updates as you type`, and a `div` with the `className` of set to the template literal `preview-box tier-${getPlayerTier(player.overallRating)}`.

Inside the last `div`, move in the `PlayerCard` component and its prop.

challenges.title: Step 38
challenges.description:
Now you will start building the form. Inside the `div` with the `className` of `form-panel`, add a `div` to wrap the "Player Info" section. Inside that new `div`, add `p` with the `className` of `form-section-title` and the text `Player Info`.

You will continue adding fields to this section in the next steps.

challenges.title: Step 39
challenges.description: Now add a `div` with the `className` of `form-group`. Inside it, add a `label` with the `className` of `label` and an `htmlFor` attribute of `name`. Under that add an `input` with an `id` set to `name`, a `className` of `input`, and a `type` attribute of `text`.

challenges.title: Step 40
challenges.description:
Proceed by adding a `div` of `className` of `form-row`. Inside it, add two `div` elements with the `className` of `form-group` each.

The first `div` element should have a `label` of `className` of `label`,  an `htmlFor` attribute of `position` a text `Position`, and an `input` with the `id` of `position`, a `className` of `input` and a `type` of `text`.

challenges.title: Step 41
challenges.description: Moving to the second `div` element, add a `label` of `className` of `label`, an `htmlFor` of `overallRating` a text of `Overall`, and an `input` with an `id` set to `overallRating`, a `className` of  of `input` and a type of `number`.

challenges.title: Step 42
challenges.description: After the `form-row`, add a `div` with the `className` of `form-group`. Inside it, add a `label` element with the `className` of `label`, an `htmlFor` of `club`, and the text `Club`. Then add an input element with an `id` of `club`, a `className` of `input`, and a `type` of `text`.

challenges.title: Step 43
challenges.description: Add another `div` with the `className` of `form-group`. Inside it, add a `label` element with the `className` of `label`, an `htmlFor` of `imageUrl`, and the text `Image URL`. Then add an `input` element with an `id` of `imageUrl`, a `className` of `input`, and a `type` of `text`.

challenges.title: Step 44
challenges.description: The Player Info section is now complete. After the closing `div` of the Player Info section and still inside `form-panel`, add a new `div`. Inside it, add a `p` element with the `className` of `form-section-title` and the text `Player Stats`. Then add a `div` with the `className` of `stats-grid`. Leave the stats grid empty for now.

challenges.title: Step 45
challenges.description:
Inside the `stats-grid`, add two `div` elements each with the `className` of `form-group`.

For the first, add a `label` with the `className` of `label`, an `htmlFor` of `pac`, and the text `PAC`, then an input with an `id` of `pac`, a `className` of `input`, and a `type` of `number`.

And for the second, add `label` with an `htmlFor` of `sho` and the text `SHO`, then an input with an `id` of `sho` and a `type` of `number`.

challenges.title: Step 46
challenges.description:
Add two more `div` elements each with the `className` of `form-group` inside `stats-grid`, one for PAS and the other for DRI.

For that of PAS, add a `label` with an `htmlFor` of `pas` and the text `PAS`, then an input with an `id` of `pas` and a `type` of `number`.

And for that of DRI, add a `label` with an `htmlFor` of `dri` and the text `DRI`, then an input with an `id` of `dri` and a `type` of `number`.

challenges.title: Step 47
challenges.description:
After the DRI `form-group`, add two more `div` elements each with the `className` of `form-group` inside `stats-grid`, one for DEF and the other for PHY.

For that of DEF, add a `label` with an `htmlFor` of `def` and the text `DEF`, then an input with an `id` of `def` and a `type` of `number`.

And for that of PHY, add a label with an `htmlFor` of `phy` and the text `PHY`, then an input with an `id` of `phy` and a `type` of `number`.

challenges.title: Step 48
challenges.description:
The form is now complete. To make the preview card update as you type, you need to connect the inputs to the `player` state. 

Starting with the Name `input`, add a `value` attribute of `player.name` to it. Then add an `onChange` attribute set to an arrow function that takes `e` as its parameter and calls `setPlayer` with a spread of `player` and `name` set to `e.target.value`. Don't forget to put both the spread and the `name` value setting in an object.

challenges.title: Step 49
challenges.description:
Now you will handle the player position selection. You have to refactor the input to a select, then map over an array of valid football positions as the value of the select options.

To start, create a `POSITIONS` array with the string values of `GK`, `CB`, `LB`, `RB`, `CDM`, `CM`, `CAM`, `LW`, `RW`, `ST`, and `CF`. Add `as const` right after the closing square brackets so Typescript narrows the type from a general `string[]` to a readonly tuple of those exact literal values.

This means TypeScript knows precisely what strings are valid, rather than just knowing it's some array of strings.

challenges.title: Step 50
challenges.description: Next, create a type `Position` with the value `typeof POSITIONS[number]`. This unpacks the `POSITIONS` array into a union of its individual string literals, effectively making it look like  `"GK" | "CB" | "LB" | ...` and so on.
tests:
const explorer = await __helpers.Explorer(code);
assert.isTrue(explorer.types.Position.matches('type Position = typeof POSITIONS[number]'));

challenges.title: Step 51
challenges.description: Change the `position` key value to the derived `Position`.

challenges.title: Step 52
challenges.description:
You will now begin the process to change the position input to a select.

Change the input to a `select` element with the id `position`, a `className` of `input`, and a `value` of `player.position`.

challenges.title: Step 53
challenges.description: Add an `onChange` attribute set to an arrow function that takes `e` as its parameter and calls `setPlayer` with a spread of `player` and `position` set to `e.target.value`, cast as `Position`.

challenges.title: Step 54
challenges.description:
Now map through the `POSITIONS` array using an arrow function that receives each position string as `pos` and returns an `option` element. The option should have a `key` of `pos` and the value also `pos`. Also, display `pos` as the text of each generated `option`.

Feel free to use anything as the param of your map, it doesn't have to be `pos`.

challenges.title: Step 55
challenges.description:
Add a `value` attribute of `player.overallRating` to the Overall `input`. After that, add an `onChange` attribute set to an arrow function that takes `e` as its parameter and calls `setPlayer` with a spread of `player` and `overallRating` set to `Number(e.target.value)`.

Since `overallRating` is a number, use `Number()` to convert the string value from the input.

challenges.title: Step 56
challenges.description: Add a `value` attribute of `player.club` to the Club `input`. After that, add an `onChange` attribute set to an arrow function that takes `e` as its parameter and calls `setPlayer` with a spread of `player` and `club` set to `e.target.value`.

challenges.title: Step 57
challenges.description: Add a `value` attribute of `player.imageUrl` to the Image URL `input`. Then set the `onChange` attribute to an arrow function that takes `e` as its parameter and calls `setPlayer` with a spread of `player` and `imageUrl` set to `e.target.value`.

challenges.title: Step 58
challenges.description: Add a `value` attribute of `player.pac` to the PAC `input`. Then add an `onChange` attribute set to an arrow function that takes `e` as its parameter and calls `setPlayer` with a spread of `player` and `pac` set to `Number(e.target.value)`.

challenges.title: Step 59
challenges.description: Add a `value` attribute of `player.sho` to the SHO `input`. Then set its `onChange` attribute to an arrow function that takes `e` as its parameter and calls `setPlayer` with a spread of `player` and `sho` set to `Number(e.target.value)`.

challenges.title: Step 60
challenges.description: Add a `value` attribute of `player.pas` to the PAS `input`. Then add an `onChange` attribute set to an arrow function that takes `e` as its parameter and calls `setPlayer` with a spread of `player` and `pas` set to `Number(e.target.value)`.

challenges.title: Step 61
challenges.description: Add a `value` attribute of `player.dri` to the DRI input. Then add an `onChange` attribute set to an arrow function that takes `e` as its parameter and calls `setPlayer` with a spread of `player` and `dri` set to `Number(e.target.value)`.

challenges.title: Step 62
challenges.description:
Add `value` and `onChange` attributes to both the DEF and PHY inputs. 

For DEF, set `value` to `player.def` and `onChange` to an arrow function that calls `setPlayer` with a spread of `player` and `def` set to `Number(e.target.value)`.

For PHY, set `value` to `player.phy` and `onChange` to an arrow function that calls `setPlayer` with a spread of `player` and `phy` set to `Number(e.target.value)`. 

With this step, all inputs are now connected to state and the live preview will update for every field!

challenges.title: Step 63
challenges.description:
The issue right now is that once the preview refreshes or you reload the page, everything you add for the player card disappears.

Saving all the inputs in `localStorage` will stop this. To begin with that, create a `STORAGE_KEY` constant with the string `football_player_card` as its value.

challenges.title: Step 64
challenges.description: Next, create a `loadPlayer` function with `PlayerData` has the return type. Inside it, add a `try...catch` syntax with `error` as the parameter of the `catch` block. Leave every other thing empty for now.

challenges.title: Step 65
challenges.description: In the `try` block, create a `saved` variable. For the value, use the `getItem()` method to retrieve `STORAGE_KEY`. Right under that variable, create an `if` statement with the condition `saved`. Leave the `if` block empty for now.

challenges.title: Step 66
challenges.description: In the block of the `if` statement, return an object spreading both `defaultPlayer` and `JSON.parse(saved)` separately. Make sure they're both separated by a comma, as they are two separate objects being merged into one.

challenges.title: Step 67
challenges.description:
In the `catch` block, log the string `Failed to load player data, using defaults:` followed by the `error` parameter to the console.

Then outside the `catch` but still within the `loadPlayer` function, return `defaultPlayer`.

All these would load the player data, populate them in the UI and load the `defaultPlayer` object if the process fails.
tests: assert.match(__helpers.removeJSComments(code), /console\.log\s*\(\s*['"`]Failed to load player data, using defaults:\s*['"`],\s*error\)/);

challenges.title: Step 68
challenges.description:
Now you will add the logic for saving to `localStorage` using `useEffect`. 

Accessing or saving data in `localStorage` is considered a side effect in React, so you should use `useEffect` for this logic.

Begin by destructuring `useEffect` from React, just as was done with `useState`.

challenges.title: Step 69
challenges.description: Next, add a `useEffect` hook that runs whenever the `player` state changes. Inside it, use an arrow function and add a `try..catch` with `error` as the parameter of the `catch` block.

challenges.title: Step 70
challenges.description:
Inside the `try` block, save the current player data to `localStorage` by calling `localStorage.setItem` with the storage key and the stringified player object.

And in the `catch` block, log the string `Failed to save player data:` followed by the `error` parameter to the console.

challenges.title: Step 71
challenges.description:
To make the localStorage feature work finally, change the state initialization from `defaultPlayer` to `loadPlayer`. This ensures the state starts with any available saved data.

Now test out the localstorage feature. Everything you enter should stick after the preview refreshes or you reload the page.

You can also change the image `src` link to `https://cdn.freecodecamp.org/curriculum/typescript/tsx-workshop/maradona.png` to see the image change in realtime. Just make sure you change the player name to Maradona.

With that, your football player card builder project is complete!

challenges.title: TypeScript Review
TypeScript Quiz
challenges.description: To pass the quiz, you must correctly answer at least 18 of the 20 questions below.

challenges.title: Build a Flashcard Quiz App
challenges.description:
In this lab, you will create an app that displays and stores flashcard data that can
be retrieved later. 

**Objective:** Fulfill the user stories below and get all the tests to pass to complete the lab.

**User Stories:**

1) You should have an HTML element with an id of `flashcard`. 
2) You should have an interface called `FlashCard`.
3) The `FlashCard` interface should contain the following properties `questionText` and `questionAnswer` both of type `string`. 
4) You should have a collection of `FlashCard` elements called `currentCards`. 
5) When the `#flashcard` element is clicked, the card should have the `flipped` class.  
6) You should have an element with an id of `delete-btn`. 
7) When the `#delete-btn` element is clicked, it should remove current card and display the previous card data. 
8) You should create an entry form with an id of `entry-form` to be able to add more flashcards to the `currentCards` collection on submit.
9) The two elements inside of the form should have an id of `front-text` and `back-text` respectively. 
10) You should create and call an `InvalidUserInputError` when either the question text or question answer is empty in the entry form.

challenges.title: Build a Digital Pet Game
challenges.description:
**Objective:** Fulfill the user stories below and get all the tests to pass to complete the lab.

**User Stories:**

The application should have two views. The requirements for each view are encompassed by the rest of the user stories:

The first view:

- Should contain a form that accepts a single string input.
- Should ask the user to name their pet.
- Should contain a button for submission that will start the game.
- Should disappear on submission.

The second view:

- Should appear upon submission of the first view.
- Should contain three `<button>` elements.
- Each button element should pertain to one each of the three following actions: `[EAT, PLAY, SLEEP]`.

  - The `EAT` button should reduce the pets `Hunger` value, and increase the pets `Energy` value.
  - The `PLAY` button should reduce the pets `Energy` value, and increase the pets `Happiness` value.
  - The `SLEEP` button should increase the pets `Hunger` value, and increase the pets `Energy` value.

- Should visually track three properties of the pet. These are: `['Hunger', 'Happiness', 'Energy']`.
- Each element that tracks each of the three properties should:

  - Contain the text relevant to that property, case insensitive, e.g., `hunger`, `Hunger`, `hUnGeR`, are all valid.
  - NOT contain the text relevant to other properties, i.e., the `Hunger` tracking element must NOT contain the text `Happiness`.
  - Contain a clear numeric representation of that properties current value.

- Each `Action` available to the pet should be encompassed by an enumerator.
- Each `Action` `button` should contain one each of the following `id`'s relevant to that button's action: `['eat-action', 'play-action', 'sleep-action']`.
- The pet should have the following moods, encompassed by a `PetMood` enumerator: `['Happy', 'Excited', 'Content', 'Sad', 'Tired', 'Sick', 'Hungry']`.

  - The pet should be `Hungry` if `Hunger > 70`.
  - The pet should be `Tired` if `Energy < 30`.
  - The pet should be `Sad` if `Happiness < 30`.
  - The pet should be `Excited` if `Happiness > 80` and `Energy > 70`.
  - The pet should be `Happy` if `Happiness > 60`.
  - Otherwise, the pet should be `Content`.

- The pet should have a number of images or emoji tied to each `Mood`.

  - These emoji or images should be tied to the current `PetMood`, with a `Record`.
  - The key of each `Record` entry should be the relevant `PetMood` enum value.
tests: assert.isNotNull(document.querySelector('form'), 'The form element does not exist at load.');
